prompt.story: Ahimsa paramo dharma — non-violence is the supreme religion. All beings desire to live; none wish to die. Recognizing this, one who truly knows the Dhamma practices non-injury in thought, word, and deed toward all living beings.

Anekantavada — the doctrine of many-sidedness — holds that reality is complex and no single perspective can capture the full truth. Syadvada, the theory of conditional predication, expresses this: from one perspective (syadasti) a thing exists; from another (syannasti) it does not; and from a third perspective both descriptions are conditionally valid.

Liberation (moksha) is attained through the Ratnatraya — the Three Jewels of Jain practice: Samyak Darshana (right faith), Samyak Jnana (right knowledge), and Samyak Charitra (right conduct). All three must be cultivated together. Faith without knowledge is blind; knowledge without conduct is barren.

The soul (jiva) is distinct from non-soul (ajiva). Karma is not metaphysical but subtly physical — fine matter that clings to the soul through attachment, passion, and activity. Through right conduct and austerity (tapas), the influx of new karma is stopped (samvara) and existing karma is shed (nirjara), leading the soul toward final liberation.

The five great vows (Mahavrata) of Jain monastics are: Ahimsa (non-violence), Satya (truth), Asteya (non-stealing), Brahmacharya (celibacy), and Aparigraha (non-possession). Laypeople observe the lesser vows (Anuvrata) to the degree their lives allow. These vows purify the soul and stop the inflow of karma.

Equanimity (samata) is the essence of Jain ethics. The wise one treats pleasure and pain, gain and loss, praise and blame with equal composure. This is not indifference but the highest self-mastery — the soul established in its own nature, unshaken by external conditions.
